past_medical_history: Mild traumatic brain injury 3 months ago (brief loss of consciousness, GCS 15 in ED, normal head CT at time of injury)
PTSD diagnosed by psychiatrist 2 months ago following traumatic building collapse in which colleague died
Mild concussion at age 22 during football, full recovery within 2 weeks
No prior psychiatric history before the incident
family_history: Father with essential tremor diagnosed in his 50s
No family history of Parkinson disease, dystonia, or Wilson disease

social_history.summary: Firefighter on medical leave for 2 months. Previously very active and physically fit. Witnessed death of his partner in a building collapse 3 months ago. Non-smoker. Social alcohol use, currently abstinent. Lives with girlfriend. No illicit drug use.

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented x4. GCS 15. Cooperative, mildly anxious on interview. MMSE 29/30 (lost 1 point on delayed recall). Mood described as 'frustrated.' No psychomotor retardation.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II-XII intact. No facial asymmetry. Visual fields full. Pupils equal and reactive.
neurological_exam.motor: Variable-amplitude right hand tremor (4-6 Hz range) that increases with attention and observation. Tremor ENTRAINS to contralateral left hand tapping (frequency shifts to match tapping rate). Intermittent fixed dystonic posturing of right hand (wrist flexion, finger extension) that resolves completely with distraction. Positive co-contraction sign on passive movement of right arm. Give-way weakness right arm 4-/5 that normalizes to 5/5 with distraction tasks. Left upper extremity 5/5 throughout. Bilateral lower extremities 5/5 with give-way pattern on right when formally tested, but normal gait pattern observed when patient asked to hurry or run.
neurological_exam.sensory: Non-dermatomal sensory loss to light touch, pinprick, and vibration affecting the entire right side of the body with sharp midline splitting (splitting of vibration sense on the sternum and forehead). Left side normal. This pattern does not correspond to any anatomical distribution.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric throughout. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Normal finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin bilaterally when patient performs tasks while distracted (e.g., counting backwards). When formally tested with attention directed to the task, right-sided dysmetria appears.
neurological_exam.gait: Right leg dragging with inconsistent foot clearance. When asked to run to catch a thrown ball, gait normalizes completely. Positive chair test (able to rise from chair without difficulty when distracted).
neurological_exam.special_signs: Positive tremor entrainment test. Positive Hoover sign on right. Positive co-contraction sign. Give-way weakness pattern. Midline splitting of vibration. Gait inconsistency with distraction.

patient.chief_complaint: Uncontrollable shaking and cramping in my right arm and leg
patient.history_present_illness: 34-year-old right-handed African American male firefighter presenting with progressive right-sided tremor and intermittent dystonic posturing of the right hand that began approximately 3 months ago, following a building collapse incident in which he sustained a mild concussion (brief loss of consciousness, GCS 15 in ED, normal head CT) and witnessed the death of his partner. The tremor was initially attributed to post-concussion syndrome by his primary care physician. Over the past 3 months, the tremor has worsened and he has developed intermittent cramping and fixed posturing of his right hand. He also reports numbness of the entire right side of his body. He notes the tremor is worse when people are watching him and seems to improve when he is distracted, such as when texting on his phone. The dystonic posturing comes and goes unpredictably. No seizure-like episodes. He has been unable to work and is on medical leave for 2 months. He reports significant nightmares, flashbacks, hypervigilance, and avoidance of fire stations. His psychiatrist diagnosed PTSD and started sertraline and prazosin.

signal_characteristics.T1: Normal cortical and subcortical signal throughout
signal_characteristics.T2: No abnormal signal in basal ganglia, thalami, or cortical gray matter
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: No white matter signal abnormality. No periventricular or juxtacortical lesions
signal_characteristics.post_gadolinium: No abnormal enhancement. No leptomeningeal enhancement

additional_observations: No evidence of contusion, diffuse axonal injury, or traumatic hemorrhage
Normal basal ganglia signal bilaterally — no evidence of Wilson disease or other metabolic deposition
Normal substantia nigra without signal abnormality
Pineal cyst is a benign incidental finding, no follow-up needed for cysts under 10 mm
mri.impression: Normal brain MRI with no evidence of traumatic brain injury sequelae, demyelination, structural lesion, or neurodegenerative process. Incidental 4 mm pineal cyst, benign. No imaging explanation for the patient's tremor and dystonic posturing. Clinical correlation recommended.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-organized, reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves and sleep spindles captured during drowsiness

artifacts.impact: Intermittent, related to jaw clenching; does not obscure interpretation

activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response, no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: Normal awake and drowsy EEG. No epileptiform discharges. Normal background activity. No focal slowing. Clinical correlation recommended.

signal_characteristics.right_striatum: Normal comma-shaped uptake with preserved caudate and putamen activity
signal_characteristics.left_striatum: Normal comma-shaped uptake with preserved caudate and putamen activity

additional_observations: Normal bilateral dopamine transporter uptake in both caudate nuclei and putamina
No asymmetric reduction in putaminal uptake
output.impression: Normal DaTscan with symmetric, preserved dopamine transporter uptake bilaterally. No evidence of nigrostriatal dopaminergic degeneration. Clinical correlation recommended.

Tremor_Accelerometry.test: Tremor frequency during contralateral ballistic movements
Tremor_Accelerometry.value: Shifts to 3.5-7.0 Hz range, entraining to contralateral tapping rate
Increased by approximately 60% when examiner watches
Tremor_Accelerometry.reference_range: No significant change in organic tremor
Unchanged or increased in organic tremor

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Tremor frequency during contralateral ballistic movements Shifts to 3.5-7.0 Hz range, entraining to contralateral tapping rate Hz (H), Tremor amplitude with direct observation Increased by approximately 60% when examiner watches relative change (H), Tremor amplitude during cognitive distraction (serial 7s) Decreased by approximately 80% relative change (H), Frequency variability coefficient 0.34  (H), Coherence with contralateral limb 0.78 during bilateral tasks  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Frequency entrainment with contralateral movement (functional tremor)

output.impression: Abnormal values: TOMM Trial 1 39/50 correct (H), TOMM Trial 2 44/50 correct (H), Reliable Digit Span 6  (L), PCL-5 (PTSD) 62 out of 80 (H), PHQ-9 (Depression) 18 out of 27 (H).

Psychiatric_Assessment.value: Witnessed death of partner in building collapse 3 months ago. Reports intrusive flashbacks of the event, avoidance of fire stations and news coverage, hypervigilance, exaggerated startle response, severe nightmares 4-5 nights per week.
Onset of tremor and dystonic symptoms within 2-3 weeks of traumatic event. Progressive worsening correlates with increasing PTSD symptom severity.
Low - passive ideation about being 'better off dead' but no plan, intent, or access to lethal means

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: PCL-5 (PTSD Checklist) 62 out of 80 (H), PHQ-9 (Depression) 18 out of 27 (H), Trauma History Witnessed death of partner in building collapse 3 months ago. Reports intrusive flashbacks of the event, avoidance of fire stations and news coverage, hypervigilance, exaggerated startle response, severe nightmares 4-5 nights per week. narrative (H), Functional Symptom Temporal Correlation Onset of tremor and dystonic symptoms within 2-3 weeks of traumatic event. Progressive worsening correlates with increasing PTSD symptom severity. narrative (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Onset of neurological symptoms temporally correlated with PTSD onset

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Functional movement disorder (functional tremor and functional dystonia) with comorbid PTSD

differential.key_distinguishing: Normal brain MRI without evidence of contusion or diffuse axonal injury. Tremor shows entrainment and distractibility, which are pathognomonic for functional tremor. Organic post-traumatic tremor has fixed frequency. Onset 3 months after a mild TBI (GCS 15, normal CT) is atypical for structural post-traumatic tremor.

Normal DaTscan definitively excludes PD. Patient's tremor is variable frequency (3.5-7 Hz) with entrainment, not the fixed 4-6 Hz pill-rolling tremor of PD. No rigidity or bradykinesia on distraction testing.

Father's essential tremor is a red herring. Patient's tremor entrains with contralateral movement, has high frequency variability (coefficient 0.34), and resolves with distraction — all features inconsistent with essential tremor, which has fixed frequency and worsens with cognitive load.
Normal ceruloplasmin (28 mg/dL). Normal MRI without basal ganglia signal changes. Age 34 is relatively late for initial presentation of Wilson disease. No Kayser-Fleischer rings on examination.

optimal_actions.action: Perform detailed neurological examination with specific attention to functional signs: tremor entrainment test, distractibility testing, Hoover sign, co-contraction sign, and consistency testing
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive tremor entrainment, positive Hoover sign, co-contraction sign, give-way weakness, midline splitting, gait inconsistency

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI to exclude structural cause including TBI sequelae, basal ganglia pathology, or demyelination
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal brain MRI with incidental pineal cyst; no evidence of TBI, structural lesion, or neurodegenerative changes

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive labs including ceruloplasmin, TSH, B12, RPR, inflammatory markers
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All normal, excluding Wilson disease, thyroid disorder, B12 deficiency, syphilis

optimal_actions.action: Order DaTscan to exclude neurodegenerative parkinsonism
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal DaTscan excluding PD and related disorders

optimal_actions.action: Consider quantitative tremor analysis to document entrainment and variability objectively
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Variable frequency tremor with entrainment and distractibility, confirming functional tremor

optimal_actions.action: Deliver the diagnosis of functional movement disorder transparently, explaining what it IS (a problem with the software of the nervous system, not the hardware), demonstrating the positive signs to the patient
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient understanding of the diagnosis with acknowledgment that symptoms are real and treatable

optimal_actions.action: Refer for specialized FND physiotherapy focusing on movement retraining and distraction-based techniques
Treat comorbid PTSD as both a precipitant and perpetuating factor — refer for trauma-focused psychotherapy (CPT or EMDR) and optimize sertraline

critical_actions: Demonstrate positive functional signs (entrainment, distractibility, Hoover sign) using validated examination techniques
Order DaTscan to exclude neurodegenerative parkinsonism
Deliver the diagnosis of FND using a transparent, evidence-based approach (explain what it IS, not just what it isn't)
Treat comorbid PTSD (which is both a precipitant and perpetuating factor)
Do NOT attribute symptoms to the mild TBI without evidence
contraindicated_actions: Labeling the patient as 'malingering' or 'faking' (FND is a genuine neurological disorder with involuntary symptom production)
Starting dopaminergic medications empirically without DaTscan confirmation
Ordering excessive additional investigations that reinforce a 'search for organic disease' narrative and delay diagnosis
Attributing symptoms to TBI without objective evidence of brain injury on MRI
key_reasoning_points: Tremor entrainment (shift in frequency with contralateral movement) is a positive diagnostic sign for functional tremor with high specificity
Variability in tremor amplitude and frequency with attention and distraction is characteristic of functional tremor; organic tremors have fixed frequency
Co-contraction sign during passive movement indicates voluntary muscle activation, a positive functional sign
Normal DaTscan excludes neurodegenerative parkinsonism, which is essential in a patient with tremor and dystonia
Give-way weakness with positive Hoover sign indicates functional weakness overlapping with the functional movement disorder
Non-dermatomal sensory loss with midline splitting (including vibration on sternum) is a positive functional sensory sign
Temporal relationship between PTSD onset and symptom onset suggests a psychological precipitant, though this is supporting evidence, not diagnostic
Mild TBI with normal MRI is a common comorbid diagnosis that can create diagnostic confusion — the absence of structural injury on MRI argues against post-traumatic movement disorder

red_herrings.data_point: Mild TBI 3 months prior with brief loss of consciousness
red_herrings.intended_effect: Attributes tremor and dystonia to structural brain injury from the concussion
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Normal brain MRI excludes structural cause; mild TBI with normal imaging does not cause progressive movement disorder. The TBI is a temporal coincidence — the psychological trauma of the event, not the physical injury, is the precipitant.

red_herrings.intended_effect: Suggests a genetic or familial tremor that the patient may have inherited
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Essential tremor is an action tremor with fixed frequency that does not entrain; patient's tremor entrains, has variable frequency, and resolves with distraction — all inconsistent with ET.

red_herrings.data_point: Incidental 4 mm pineal cyst on MRI
red_herrings.intended_effect: May distract the clinician toward a structural cause for symptoms
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Pineal cysts under 10 mm are extremely common benign incidental findings present in up to 10% of MRIs and have no clinical significance.

metadata.diagnostic_challenge: Functional movement disorder (tremor + dystonia) in a young firefighter with comorbid mild TBI and PTSD. The TBI history and family history of essential tremor create strong diagnostic anchors toward organic disease. Requires recognition of positive functional signs and DaTscan to exclude neurodegeneration.
